title: Elena Cruz — First headache visit
description: 9-year-old with 4 months of recurrent headaches, now 2-3x/week. One ER visit in March (no imaging). Migraine-compatible pattern, school impact, no red flags.

history.label: Well-child visit note
history.text: 9-year-old female, healthy. Term birth, no complications. Mild intermittent asthma, albuterol PRN, well controlled. Immunizations up to date. No prior hospitalizations or surgeries. No headache complaints at this visit. Normal growth and development, normal neuro screening exam.

history.label: Outside ER visit note
history.text: Presented with acute headache and vomiting. Treated with IV fluids and observation. No imaging obtained; discharged same day, improved. Note references a head CT order — no report found in records. PCP follow-up recommended.

history.label: Vitals (today)
history.text: BP 98/62, HR 84, T 36.9C, Ht 133 cm, Wt 29 kg. No fever. Appears well.

history.label: Medication list & allergies
history.text: No daily medications. PRN ibuprofen per parent report at check-in — minimal relief, inconsistent dosing. PRN albuterol inhaler (mild intermittent asthma). Allergies: no known drug allergies.

history.label: Intake form
history.text: Headaches about 4 months, now 2-3 times a week per parent. OTC ibuprofen tried with minimal relief. No prior neurology contact. Mother: migraine with aura. Red-flag screen items all answered no.

red_flag_catalog.label: Sudden severe (thunderclap) / worst headache of life
Wakes from sleep / worst on awakening
Vision changes (double vision, non-aura vision loss)
Fever, stiff neck, or systemic infection signs
History of cancer, immune suppression, or shunt

pedmidas_questions.question: Full school days missed in last 3 months
Partial school days missed in last 3 months
School days functioning at less than half
Days unable to do home activities/chores
Days participated in activities at less than half

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Hi Elena, hi mom. Can you tell me when the headaches started?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Around March. She had one bad one that sent us to the ER — the fluids helped. They watched her for a few hours and sent us home, no scans or anything.

evidence.quote: Around March. She had one bad one that sent us to the ER — the fluids helped. They watched her for a few hours and sent us home, no scans or anything.

repeat_visits.value: 1 ER visit for acute headache with vomiting (Mar 2, 2026) — IV fluids, no imaging

missing_questions: How many headache days per month?
How long does each headache last?
Where does it hurt and what does it feel like?
Any nausea, vomiting, light or sound sensitivity?
Any headaches that wake her from sleep?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: And how often is she getting them now?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Two, sometimes three times a week. Usually after school.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: And how long does one usually last?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: A couple of hours, sometimes the rest of the evening until she falls asleep.

evidence.quote: Two, sometimes three times a week. Usually after school.
A couple of hours, sometimes the rest of the evening until she falls asleep.

episode_duration.value: Hours (sometimes the rest of the evening until sleep)

missing_questions_remove: How many headache days per month?
How long does each headache last?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Elena, where does it hurt, and what does it feel like?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Right here, across my forehead. Both sides. It pounds, like a drum.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: If one comes at soccer, does running around make it worse?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Yeah, it pounds harder when I run. I have to sit down.

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Maybe once a month there's a really bad one where she just can't do anything at all.

evidence.quote: Right here, across my forehead. Both sides. It pounds, like a drum.
Yeah, it pounds harder when I run. I have to sit down.
Maybe once a month there's a really bad one where she just can't do anything at all.

activity_worsening.value: Worse with routine physical activity (running at soccer)

missing_questions_remove: Where does it hurt and what does it feel like?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Any nausea, or does light bother you when it happens?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Yeah — the lights in the classroom are the worst. Sometimes I feel sick.

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: She'll ask to lie down in a dark room.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Elena, right before a headache starts, do you ever see flashing lights, zigzag lines, spots — or get numb or tingly anywhere?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: No, nothing like that. It just starts hurting.

evidence.quote: Yeah — the lights in the classroom are the worst. Sometimes I feel sick.
She'll ask to lie down in a dark room.
No, nothing like that. It just starts hurting.

aura.value: No visual or sensory aura reported

missing_questions_remove: Any nausea, vomiting, light or sound sensitivity?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Some important safety questions. Any early-morning headaches, vomiting on waking, or vision changes?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: No — the ER vomiting was during the headache, not waking up. No vision problems, and nothing in the mornings.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Do headaches ever wake her from sleep? Any weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, or a sudden worst-headache-of-her-life moment?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Never at night — they're always afternoon or evening. No weakness or numbness, her speech is fine, and nothing sudden — they build up slowly.

evidence.quote: No — the ER vomiting was during the headache, not waking up. No vision problems, and nothing in the mornings.
Never at night — they're always afternoon or evening. No weakness or numbness, her speech is fine, and nothing sudden — they build up slowly.

missing_questions_remove: Any headaches that wake her from sleep?
missing_questions_add: Seizures, gait or balance changes, fever/stiff neck, head trauma?
Is the overall pattern stable or worsening month to month?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Any seizures or staring spells? Changes in her balance or how she walks? Times she seemed confused or not herself? And does coughing, straining, or hard exercise ever set one off suddenly?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: No seizures, her balance is fine, and she's always herself. Coughing or straining has never started one.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Any fevers or a stiff neck with these? Any big hits to the head recently? And she's never had cancer, immune problems, or a shunt, right?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: No fevers or stiff neck, no head injuries, and none of that medical history — she's been healthy.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Compared to March, are they getting worse month to month, or about the same?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: They picked up in the spring, but the last couple of months it's been steady. Not really worse.

evidence.quote: No seizures, her balance is fine, and she's always herself. Coughing or straining has never started one.
No fevers or stiff neck, no head injuries, and none of that medical history — she's been healthy.
They picked up in the spring, but the last couple of months it's been steady. Not really worse.

progression.value: Rose in spring, steady the last 2 months — non-progressive currently

missing_questions_remove: Seizures, gait or balance changes, fever/stiff neck, head trauma?
Is the overall pattern stable or worsening month to month?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Let's talk about school. In the last three months, how many full days has she missed because of headaches? And days she went but left early?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Full days missed — I'd say 3. Partial days, maybe 5 more.

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Some days I'm at school but my head hurts too much to do my work — maybe 4 days like that.

evidence.quote: Full days missed — I'd say 3. Partial days, maybe 5 more.
Some days I'm at school but my head hurts too much to do my work — maybe 4 days like that.

school_impact.value: Missed 3 full days, 5 partial days, ~4 low-function days in 3 months

missing_questions_add: Days unable to do chores or home activities (PedMIDAS)
Days missing or limited in sports/social activities (PedMIDAS)

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Outside school — days she couldn't do her normal things at home? And days she skipped soccer or playing with friends, or went but wasn't really herself?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Maybe 2 days she went straight to bed and did nothing at home. She skipped soccer twice, and probably 3 more times she played but had to come off early.

evidence.quote: Maybe 2 days she went straight to bed and did nothing at home. She skipped soccer twice, and probably 3 more times she played but had to come off early.

activity_impact.value: Skipped soccer 2x, limited participation 3x; ~2 days no home activities in 3 months

missing_questions_remove: Days unable to do chores or home activities (PedMIDAS)
Days missing or limited in sports/social activities (PedMIDAS)
missing_questions_add: Sleep schedule, hydration, meals, screen time
What medications is she taking for attacks and how often?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: What about sleep and screens at home? Water, caffeine?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Bedtime moves around — she gets about eight hours. She's on the tablet a lot in the evenings. She doesn't drink much water, and no caffeine at all.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: And what does she take when a headache hits?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: Ibuprofen, but honestly it barely helps. We're not consistent with the dose — maybe once or twice a week she gets some.

evidence.quote: Bedtime moves around — she gets about eight hours. She's on the tablet a lot in the evenings. She doesn't drink much water, and no caffeine at all.
Ibuprofen, but honestly it barely helps. We're not consistent with the dose — maybe once or twice a week she gets some.
No daily medications. PRN ibuprofen per parent report at check-in — minimal relief, inconsistent dosing.

treatment_response.value: Minimal relief from ibuprofen; dosing inconsistent — dose/timing not yet optimized

medication_overuse_risk.value: Ibuprofen ~1-2 days/week (~6 days/month) — below the >=10 days/month overuse threshold

missing_questions_remove: Sleep schedule, hydration, meals, screen time
What medications is she taking for attacks and how often?

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Family history of migraines?

turns.speaker: parent
turns.text: I get migraines with aura. Have for years.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Her exam today is reassuring — she's well-appearing, and her neuro exam is completely normal: eye movements, strength, coordination and gait all fine. I got a good look at her optic discs and there's no papilledema.

turns.speaker: clinician
turns.text: Putting it together, this looks most like migraine — my concern level today is low. Plan: start a headache diary, work on regular sleep, evening screens and hydration, keep ibuprofen to no more than two days a week but give a proper dose early in the attack, and we set her PedMIDAS baseline today. No imaging is needed today, and I'll see her back in eight weeks.

evidence.quote: I get migraines with aura. Have for years.
BP 98/62, HR 84, T 36.9C, Ht 133 cm, Wt 29 kg. No fever. Appears well.
she's well-appearing, and her neuro exam is completely normal: eye movements, strength, coordination and gait all fine. I got a good look at her optic discs and there's no papilledema.
this looks most like migraine — my concern level today is low. Plan: start a headache diary, work on regular sleep, evening screens and hydration, keep ibuprofen to no more than two days a week

summary: 9-year-old with 4 months of recurrent bifrontal pounding headaches, 2-3 days/week (~10 days/month), lasting hours; frequency rose in spring, steady the last 2 months. [ev-1, ev-2, ev-3, ev-4, ev-9]
Phenotype: worse with routine activity, photophobia and nausea with dark-room seeking, no aura; family history of migraine with aura (mother). [ev-4b, ev-5, ev-5b, ev-5c, ev-14]

Red-flag screen negative today across all 14 items: no thunderclap, waking headaches, morning vomiting, focal deficits, vision or speech change, seizures, gait change, altered mental status, fever/stiff neck, trauma, or progressive worsening; normal vitals and exam, no papilledema. [ev-6, ev-7, ev-8, ev-8b, ev-9, ev-15, ev-16]
Functional burden: PedMIDAS 19 (mild disability) — 3 full + 5 partial school days missed, ~4 low-function school days, soccer skipped/limited 5x; one ER visit in March. [ev-10, ev-10b, ev-11, ev-1]

Treatment response: OTC ibuprofen with minimal relief and inconsistent dosing at ~1-2 days/week — below overuse threshold; lifestyle contributors present (variable bedtime, high evening screens, low hydration). [ev-13, ev-13b, ev-12]
Unresolved record: the outside ER note references a head CT order but no report is on file — report requested from the ER, reconcile before any imaging decision. [emr-ev-2]
questions_to_ask: Confirm weight-based ibuprofen dose (10 mg/kg) and counsel on taking it early in the attack
Confirm whether any head CT was actually performed at the March ER visit
suggested_pathway: Discuss migraine-compatible feature pattern with family (clinician review required)
Start structured headache diary with family (draft provided)
Lifestyle plan: consistent bedtime, evening screen limits, daily hydration goal
Acute strategy: proper-dose ibuprofen early in attack, capped at 2 days/week
PedMIDAS baseline 19 recorded today — re-score at 8-week follow-up
Consider preventive-pathway discussion if frequency/disability persists at follow-up
referral_considerations: No urgent neurology referral indicated today based on negative red-flag screen (clinician to confirm)
Refer to neurology if red flags emerge, the pattern progresses, or frequency exceeds ~4/month despite 8-12 weeks of the initial plan
Reconcile the referenced ER head CT order before any new imaging decision
patient_instructions: Keep the headache diary daily — one tap per day
Same bedtime every night; tablet away an hour before bed
Carry a water bottle to school and drink through the day
Give ibuprofen at the right dose early in an attack, but not more than 2 days per week
Return sooner if headaches wake her at night, come with vomiting in the morning, or change suddenly
care_plan.disclaimer: Draft for clinician review. Final clinical decisions remain with the clinician.

canned_qa.answer: The family reported Elena missed 3 full school days and left early 5 times in the last 3 months, plus about 4 days where she was at school but couldn't do her work. This is captured in PedMIDAS items 1-3 (baseline total 19, mild disability).

No SNOOP red flags identified on the intake questionnaire — no systemic symptoms, focal deficits, thunderclap onset, or morning vomiting reported. Today's interview confirmed all 14 screened red flags as absent, including waking headaches, seizures, gait change, and progressive worsening. This remains a clinician-review screen, not a diagnosis.

Mar 2, 2026: acute headache with vomiting, treated with IV fluids and observation. No imaging was obtained; discharged same day. Note: the ER record references a head CT order, but no report has been found — it has been requested from the ER.

No head imaging report is on file. The March ER note references a head CT order, but no report was found in records — it has been requested from the ER for reconciliation. With today's negative red-flag screen and normal exam, no imaging trigger is currently met; this remains a clinician decision.

From the chart: term birth with no complications, mild intermittent asthma managed with PRN albuterol (no headache contraindication identified), immunizations up to date, and no known drug allergies. No daily medications.

Per parent report, Elena gets OTC ibuprofen about 1-2 days per week with minimal relief and inconsistent dosing — below the >=10 days/month overuse threshold. The draft plan suggests a proper weight-based dose taken early in the attack, capped at 2 days/week (clinician review required).
The mother reports longstanding migraine with aura, confirmed during today's visit and on the intake form. No vision or hearing concerns and normal growth and development are noted for Elena.
